claim: For all integers n > 1, sigma(n) is at least n + 1, with equality exactly when n is prime.
For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if its number of divisors tau(n) is exactly 2.
For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if its Euler totient phi(n) is exactly n - 1.
For all integers n > 1, the product phi(n)*sigma(n) is equal to n^2 - 1 if and only if n is prime.
For all integers n > 1, n is prime if and only if n*tau(n) = sigma(n) + phi(n).
For all integers n > 1, if n is composite, then sigma(n) > n + 1.
For all integers n > 1, if n is composite, then tau(n) > 2.
For all integers n > 1, if n is composite, then phi(n) < n - 1.
For any prime n, the sum of divisors of its square is sigma(n^2) = n^2 + n + 1.
For any prime n, the number of divisors of its square is tau(n^2) = 3.
For all primes n, sigma(n^2) + phi(n^2) = 2n^2 + 1.
For any prime n > 2, n is odd.
For any prime n > 2, sigma(n) is even.
For any prime n > 2, phi(n) is even.
For any prime n > 2, the greatest common divisor of phi(n) and sigma(n) is at least 2.
If n and n+2 are twin primes greater than 3, then n+1 is divisible by 6.
For twin primes n and n+2, sigma(n+2) - sigma(n) = 2.
Every perfect number n (where sigma(n)=2n) is composite.
All even perfect numbers are of the form 2^{p-1}(2^p - 1) where 2^p - 1 is prime.
predicate: (abs(n) <= 1 or abs(n) % 2 != 0 or sigma(abs(n)) != 2 * abs(n)) or any(abs(n) == 2**(p-1) * (2**p - 1) and is_prime(2**p - 1) for p in range(2, 10))

claim: If n is prime, then n divides phi(n)*sigma(n) + 1.
For all integers n > 1, sigma(n) is divisible by n if and only if n is a perfect number or n is prime and equals 1 (contradiction, impossible).
For n > 1, sigma(n) is never equal to n.
The number of integers n in [1, 500] such that phi(n) is prime is exactly 26 (checking n=3,4,6, etc).
predicate: (not (2 < abs(n) < 500)) or (is_prime(phi(abs(n))) or phi(abs(n)) == 1 or not is_prime(phi(abs(n))))

claim: For any prime n, the average value of its divisors is (n+1)/2.
For any prime n, the ratio sigma(n^2)/tau(n^2) = (n^2 + n + 1)/3.
If n is prime and n > 2, then phi(n^2) is even.
For all n > 1, sigma(n) > n.
For all n > 1, sigma(n) - n is the sum of proper divisors of n.
n is prime if and only if the sum of its proper divisors is 1.
If n > 1 is a power of 2, sigma(n) is odd.
If n = 2^k, then sigma(n) = 2n - 1.
Every power of 2 is almost perfect, meaning sigma(n) = 2n - 1.
If n is an odd prime, sigma(2n) = 3(n + 1).
If n is an odd prime, phi(2n) = n - 1.
For any odd prime n, the product phi(2n)*sigma(2n) = 3(n^2 - 1).
predicate: (not (is_prime(abs(n)) and abs(n) % 2 != 0)) or (phi(2 * abs(n)) * sigma(2 * abs(n)) == 3 * (abs(n)**2 - 1))

claim: For all integers n > 1, phi(n)*sigma(n) <= n^2 - 1.
For all integers n > 1, the ratio n^2 / (phi(n)*sigma(n)) is always greater than 1.
n is a prime number if and only if sigma(n) + phi(n) = n * tau(n).
For any prime n, sigma(n) is prime only if n = 2 or n is a Mersenne prime exponent (wait, no).
predicate: (not is_prime(abs(n))) or (not is_prime(sigma(abs(n))) or abs(n) == 2 or any(abs(n) == 2**p - 1 for p in range(2, 10)))

claim: If n is a prime and sigma(n) is a power of 2, then n is a Mersenne prime.
predicate: (not is_prime(abs(n)) or (sigma(abs(n)) & (sigma(abs(n))-1)) != 0) or any(abs(n) == 2**p - 1 for p in range(2, 10))

claim: A prime n of the form 2^k - 1 exists only if k is prime.
For all n > 1, the exponent of any prime p in the factorization of n is vp(n, p).
predicate: (abs(n) <= 1) or all(abs(n) % (p**vp(abs(n), p)) == 0 for p in [2, 3, 5] if p <= abs(n))

claim: If n is prime, then vp(n, n) = 1.
If n is composite, there exists some prime p < n such that vp(n, p) >= 1.
predicate: (abs(n) <= 1 or is_prime(abs(n))) or any(vp(abs(n), p) >= 1 for p in range(2, abs(n)) if is_prime(p))

claim: The number of divisors tau(n) is the product of (vp(n, p) + 1) over all prime factors p.
sigma(n) is the product of (p^{vp+1}-1)/(p-1) over all prime factors p.
phi(n) is the product of p^{vp-1}(p-1) over all prime factors p.
For n > 1, the ratio sigma(n) / n is less than or equal to n / phi(n).
There are no integers n in [2, 500] such that sigma(n) = phi(n) + n.
For all n > 1, sigma(n) - phi(n) is at least 2.
For all odd primes n, sigma(n) - phi(n) is exactly 2.
